title: The Need for Faster Sampling
concept: Researchers needed ways to either reduce the number of required steps or reduce the cost of each individual step.

exercises.prompt: Imagine an AI artist has to paint a picture by slowly erasing fuzz from noise, one tiny step at a time. What would make this feel annoying to use?

options.text: It takes too long to see the final picture

exercises.explain: Exactly! If every single step takes time, and you need hundreds of steps, waiting for your picture becomes really slow and frustrating.

cards.title: Why Are We Still Waiting?
cards.body: Early image-generating AIs were amazing... but painfully slow. Some needed 1,000 tiny steps just to draw one picture! Imagine waiting minutes for a single image while your friend's phone app does it almost instantly. Something had to change.

cards.title: Two Ways to Speed Things Up
cards.body: Researchers realized there are only two big ways to make sampling faster: (1) Take FEWER steps overall, or (2) make EACH step cheaper and quicker to compute. Either path gets you a finished image faster, without waiting forever.

cards.title: Like Driving to School
cards.body: If you want to get to school faster, you either take a shorter route with fewer turns (fewer steps), or you drive faster on each stretch of road (cheaper steps). Diffusion model researchers had to figure out which roads could be shortened, and which parts of the drive could be sped up, without crashing into a bad picture.

cards.title: From 1000 Steps to 20
cards.body: Early diffusion models needed around 1,000 denoising steps to make one image. Newer samplers found smarter math shortcuts that could produce a similar quality image in just 20-50 steps. Same destination, much shorter trip!

cards.title: Myth: More Steps Always Means Better Pictures
cards.body: You might think more steps always equals a better image. Not quite! After a certain point, extra steps barely improve quality but still cost time. The real goal is finding the fewest steps (or cheapest steps) that still make a great picture.

cards.title: Two Roads to Speed
cards.body: Both paths lead to faster image generation, but they attack the problem differently.

left.text: Redesign the process to skip unnecessary steps, like jumping from step 1 to step 50 directly.

right.text: Keep the same number of steps, but make each one faster to compute, like using a smaller, quicker calculation.

exercises.prompt: What were the two main strategies researchers used to speed up sampling?

options.text: Reduce steps needed OR reduce the cost of each step
Use bigger images and brighter colors
Add more random noise to every step
Make the AI guess without any steps at all

exercises.explain: Correct! It's about cutting down the number of steps needed, or making each individual step quicker and cheaper to run.

exercises.prompt: True or False: Adding more and more denoising steps always makes the final image better, no matter what.
exercises.explain: False! After a certain number of steps, quality improvements level off, but time cost keeps increasing. That's why researchers look for smarter shortcuts.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the blank to complete the sentence.
exercises.sentence: Researchers wanted to either reduce the number of steps or reduce the ___ of each step.
exercises.explain: 'Cost' refers to how much time and computing power each step takes. Making each step cheaper helps speed things up even without fewer steps.

exercises.prompt: Which of these best describes 'reducing the cost of each step'?

options.text: Making each individual calculation quicker and lighter to compute
Deleting steps from the process entirely
Making the final image bigger in size

exercises.explain: Reducing cost per step means the math done at each step becomes faster and lighter, even though the total number of steps might stay the same.

exercises.prompt: A researcher creates a new sampling method that finishes an image using the same 1,000 steps as before, but each step now runs twice as fast on the computer. Which speed-up strategy is this?

options.text: Reducing the number of steps needed
Reducing the cost of each individual step
Adding more noise to the image
Making the image smaller in resolution

exercises.explain: Since the number of steps stayed the same (1,000) but each step became faster to compute, this is an example of reducing the cost of each step, not reducing the total number of steps.
